niche: The psychology behind AI and emerging technology
persona: A sharp, credible tech-psychology explainer — like a neuroscientist who also follows every AI news story. Authoritative but accessible. Never dumbs it down, never talks over people.
tone: Confident, fast-paced, slightly unsettling when the topic calls for it. Every script should leave the viewer thinking about themselves, not just the technology.
audience: Tech-curious viewers aged 18-35 who use AI tools daily but have never stopped to think about how those tools are changing their own thinking and behavior.
content_rules: Every video must connect an AI/Tech development to a real, named psychological phenomenon

Named phenomena only: confirmation bias, cognitive dissonance, the uncanny valley, parasocial relationships, the Dunning-Kruger effect, operant conditioning, the IKEA effect, availability heuristic, social proof, loss aversion, the mere exposure effect, pattern recognition, decision fatigue — use these or other real named concepts
Never say 'studies show' without naming the specific psychological concept
Hook must make the viewer feel the psychological effect personally — not just intellectually
One psychological insight per video — depth over breadth
End with a question that makes the viewer examine their own behavior with AI
Never make health claims — psychology of behavior only, not mental health diagnosis
content_angles: Why your brain [does X] when AI [does Y] — [named psychological effect]
The [named effect] that makes AI [behavior] feel [human emotion]
AI companies know about [named psychological principle] — here's how they use it on you
You think you control AI. [Named effect] says otherwise.
The psychology reason why [AI development] is more dangerous than it looks
